content.problemStatement:
You have a grid with NNN rows and MMM columns in which KKK cells are black and the rest are white.

Let (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) represent the cell in the iii-th row from the top and the jjj-th column from the left.

If you are currently at cell (i,j)(i, j)(i,j), you can:


move one cell down to (i+1,j)(i + 1, j)(i+1,j)
move one cell right to (i,j+1)(i, j + 1)(i,j+1)
The cost of a path is defined as the number of times you move between two cells that have different colours.

You are given QQQ queries. In the iii-th query, you are initially at (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) and want to reach (Xi,Yi)(X_i, Y_i)(Xi​,Yi​) with the minimum cost.

For each query, before you begin, you can perform the following operation any number of times (possibly zero):


Choose a column and flip the colour of all cells in this column (i.e. change the colour of all the black cells to white and the colour of all the white cells to black in this column)
For each query, what is the minimum cost of a path from (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1) to (Xi,Yi)(X_i, Y_i)(Xi​,Yi​) after performing this operation any number of times (possibly zero)?

**Note:** The operations applied in different queries are independent of each other.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains three integers NNN, MMM, KKK and QQQ - the number of rows, the number of columns, the number of black cells and the number of queries respectively.
The following KKK lines each contain two integers AiA_iAi​ and BiB_iBi​ - indicating that the iii-th black cell is at (Ai,Bi)(A_i, B_i)(Ai​,Bi​).
The following QQQ lines each contain two integers XiX_iXi​ and YiY_iYi​ - the destination cell of the iii-th query.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing QQQ space separated integers ― the answer for each of the QQQ queries.
constraints: the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 6⋅1056 \cdot 10^56⋅105
the sum of MMM over all test cases does not exceed 6⋅1056 \cdot 10^56⋅105
the sum of KKK over all test cases does not exceed 6⋅1056 \cdot 10^56⋅105
the sum of QQQ over all test cases does not exceed 6⋅1056 \cdot 10^56⋅105

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1:

Query 1: We are already at (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1), so the minimum cost is 000.

Test case 2:

Initial Grid:




Query 1: The only sequence of possible moves is (1,1)→black to whitecost = 1(2,1)(1, 1) \xrightarrow[\text{black to white}]{\text{cost = } 1} (2, 1)(1,1)cost = 1black to white​(2,1). So the total cost is 111.


Query 2: The only sequence of possible moves is (1,1)→black to whitecost = 1(2,1)→white to blackcost = 1(3,1)(1, 1) \xrightarrow[\text{black to white}]{\text{cost = } 1} (2, 1) \xrightarrow[\text{white to black}]{\text{cost = } 1} (3, 1)(1,1)cost = 1black to white​(2,1)cost = 1white to black​(3,1). So the cost is 222.


It can be shown that no better answer is possible by performing any column flipping operations.
Test case 3:

Initial Grid:



Query 1: One optimal solution is to flip columns 222 and 444 to obtain the following grid.


Then you can perform the following moves for a cost of 000 -  (1,1)→white to whitecost = 0(1,2)→white to whitecost = 0(1,3)→white to whitecost = 0(1,4)(1, 1) \xrightarrow[\text{white to white}]{\text{cost = } 0} (1, 2) \xrightarrow[\text{white to white}]{\text{cost = } 0} (1, 3) \xrightarrow[\text{white to white}]{\text{cost = } 0} (1, 4)(1,1)cost = 0white to white​(1,2)cost = 0white to white​(1,3)cost = 0white to white​(1,4)

Query 2: One optimal solution is to flip columns 222.


Then you can perform the following moves for a cost of 000 - (1,1)→white to whitecost = 0(1,2)→white to whitecost = 0(1,3)→white to whitecost = 0(2,3)→white to whitecost = 0(2,4)→white to whitecost = 0(3,4)(1, 1) \xrightarrow[\text{white to white}]{\text{cost = } 0} (1, 2) \xrightarrow[\text{white to white}]{\text{cost = } 0} (1, 3) \xrightarrow[\text{white to white}]{\text{cost = } 0} (2, 3) \xrightarrow[\text{white to white}]{\text{cost = } 0} (2, 4) \xrightarrow[\text{white to white}]{\text{cost = } 0} (3, 4)(1,1)cost = 0white to white​(1,2)cost = 0white to white​(1,3)cost = 0white to white​(2,3)cost = 0white to white​(2,4)cost = 0white to white​(3,4)

Query 3: One optimal solution is to flip no columns and then perform the following moves for a cost of 111 - (1,1)→white to blackcost = 1(2,1)(1, 1) \xrightarrow[\text{white to black}]{\text{cost = } 1} (2, 1)(1,1)cost = 1white to black​(2,1) for a total cost of 111.

Test case 4:

Initial Grid:




Query 1: One optimal solution is to flip no columns and then perform the following moves for a cost of 222 -  (1,1)→white to blackcost = 1(2,1)→black to whitecost = 1(3,1)→white to whitecost = 0(4,1)(1, 1) \xrightarrow[\text{white to black}]{\text{cost = } 1} (2, 1) \xrightarrow[\text{black to white}]{\text{cost = } 1} (3, 1) \xrightarrow[\text{white to white}]{\text{cost = } 0} (4, 1)(1,1)cost = 1white to black​(2,1)cost = 1black to white​(3,1)cost = 0white to white​(4,1)


Query 2: One optimal solution is to flip no columns and then perform the following moves for a cost of 111 -  (1,1)→white to blackcost = 1(2,1)→black to blackcost = 0(2,2)→black to blackcost = 0(3,2)→black to blackcost = 0(4,2)(1, 1) \xrightarrow[\text{white to black}]{\text{cost = } 1} (2, 1) \xrightarrow[\text{black to black}]{\text{cost = } 0} (2, 2) \xrightarrow[\text{black to black}]{\text{cost = } 0} (3, 2) \xrightarrow[\text{black to black}]{\text{cost = } 0} (4, 2)(1,1)cost = 1white to black​(2,1)cost = 0black to black​(2,2)cost = 0black to black​(3,2)cost = 0black to black​(4,2)
